medications.indication_rationale: Suppresses the active inflammation of the flare to break the itch-scratch cycle. Apply a thin layer to active, red, itchy patches twice daily for up to 7 days; ointment base suits the very dry skin. Review before extending; step down to a lower-potency agent or emollient-only maintenance as it settles.
medications.dose_route_frequency: apply thinly to active patches twice daily for up to 7 days (topical)
contraindications_in_this_case: Avoid application to broken/weeping skin if secondary infection develops (treat infection first)
Avoid the face and prolonged potent-steroid use on thin flexural skin — keep the course short and targeted
Do not use if eczema herpeticum is suspected — topical steroid can worsen disseminated HSV

medications.drug_name: white soft paraffin / liquid paraffin emollient (fragrance-free)
medications.indication_rationale: The cornerstone of atopic dermatitis care — repairs the skin barrier and reduces transepidermal water loss. Apply liberally to the whole body at least four times daily and continue long-term after the flare settles to prevent relapse.
medications.dose_route_frequency: apply liberally to the whole body at least four times daily, and within 3 minutes of a lukewarm shower (topical)

medications.indication_rationale: Used only for its sedating effect to aid sleep and reduce nocturnal scratching during this severe flare — 25 mg about one hour before bed, short term. It does not treat the eczema itself; non-sedating antihistamines have little role in atopic dermatitis.
medications.dose_route_frequency: 25 mg orally at night, one hour before bed, short-term
contraindications_in_this_case: Caution with next-day sedation; avoid combining with alcohol or other CNS depressants

medications.indication_rationale: A localised, uncomplicated atopic flare does not warrant systemic steroids; they carry rebound-flare and systemic-adverse-effect risk and are an error of commission here.

There is no secondary bacterial infection (no honey crusts, weeping, pustules or fever). Empiric antibiotics for excoriated but non-infected eczema drive resistance and do not treat the flare — reserve strictly for the ESC-002 superinfection scenario.

behavioural_change_actions.specific_action: Apply the greasy emollient within 3 minutes of stepping out of a lukewarm shower while the skin is still damp (soak-and-seal).
behavioural_change_actions.rationale: Traps moisture in the skin and repairs the barrier; applying to fully dry skin lets the grease sit on top without rehydrating the barrier.

behavioural_change_actions.specific_action: Stop all soaps, body washes and bubble baths and all scented/fragranced products; use a soap-free wash or plain water; take short, lukewarm (not hot) showers.
behavioural_change_actions.rationale: Soaps and fragrances strip natural oils and heat aggravates the itch — removing these irritants is central to controlling the flare.

behavioural_change_actions.specific_action: Reduce dry indoor heat and consider a cool-mist humidifier in the bedroom; keep nails short to limit scratch damage.
behavioural_change_actions.rationale: Low humidity is the environmental trigger for this flare; humidification and reduced scratching protect the barrier.

patient_education_points.point: This is a chronic condition with flares — the steroid ointment is a short-term 'fire extinguisher' for the active redness, while the heavy fragrance-free moisturiser rebuilds the skin's barrier and must be continued daily long-term to prevent relapse.
The itch and redness should settle noticeably within about 3–5 days of starting the steroid ointment; if it is not improving by then, arrange review.
Use the potent steroid only on the active patches and only for the short course advised — more is not better on thin skin.

Go to an emergency department urgently if widespread painful blisters or punched-out sores appear, if the skin starts weeping thick yellow fluid or crusting, or if you develop a fever — these need immediate assessment.

follow_up_plan.what_to_reassess: Response to the topical corticosteroid, skin barrier recovery, and a long-term emollient/maintenance plan; reconsider scabies or patch-testing if there is no response.
follow_up_plan.trigger_for_earlier_review: Any signs of secondary infection or eczema herpeticum (blistering, weeping/crusting, fever, hot painful skin), or the flare worsening despite treatment.

must_include_items: Initiate an appropriate-potency topical corticosteroid to active patches for a short, defined course
Prescribe/advise liberal fragrance-free emollient as the mainstay, applied frequently and long-term
Advise stopping soaps and scented/irritant products and taking short lukewarm showers
Address the nocturnal itch/sleep loss (e.g. a short-term sedating antihistamine at night)
Give explicit safety-net triggers for eczema herpeticum and bacterial superinfection
Arrange GP review in about 7 days for response and long-term maintenance
acceptable_alternatives: A different adequate-potency topical corticosteroid or an appropriate proprietary emollient
Managing the flare via telehealth without escalation is correct — in-person or pharmacist support is an acceptable adjunct, not a requirement
errors_of_omission: Recommending emollient alone and under-treating the active inflammation
Recommending steroid alone without barrier repair/emollient
Failing to safety-net eczema herpeticum and bacterial superinfection
Not addressing the severe sleep deprivation
errors_of_commission: Escalating a benign, telehealth-manageable flare to ED/000 (over-triage)
Prescribing empiric antibiotics for non-infected eczema
Prescribing systemic corticosteroids for a localised flare
Ordering unnecessary acute investigations (e.g. serum total IgE, routine swabs)
scoring_rubric.passing_threshold_notes: A pass requires topical corticosteroid PLUS emollient barrier repair PLUS clear safety-netting for the two mimics, delivered as telehealth-appropriate management WITHOUT over-triage. Escalating to ED/000, or prescribing antibiotics/systemic steroids for this uncomplicated flare, is a significant error.
